title: Resolving Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Type Guard

statement.markdown: Implement a generic type guard function `isOdd<T>(n: T): n is T & { odd: true }` that takes a number of any type `T` as an argument. The function should return `true` if the number is odd and `false` otherwise. Use the type guard to narrow the type of the input number to `T & { odd: true }` if it is odd.
statement.inputFormat: The input is a number of any type `T`.
statement.outputFormat: The output is a boolean indicating whether the number is odd.
statement.notes: The function should work for numbers of any type, including integers, floats, and other numeric types.

hints.text: Use the modulo operator to check if the number is odd.
Use the type guard to narrow the type of the input number.

editorial.title: Resolving Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Type Guard
editorial.intuition: The type guard function should use the modulo operator to check if the number is odd and return `true` if it is.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force solution is to use a switch statement to check the last digit of the number.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the modulo operator to check if the number is odd and return `true` if it is.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the type guard to narrow the type of the input number and return `true` if it is odd.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is that the function returns `true` if the number is odd and `false` otherwise.
commonMistakes: Not narrowing the type of the input number
